did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a hybrid deep feature extraction approach combining handcrafted features (HOG, LBP) and deep learning features (DenseNet201, ResNet50) for skin cancer detection from dermoscopy images. These features were classified using machine learning and deep learning models including SVM, Random Forest, Artificial Neural Network (ANN), XGBoost, and LightGBM to distinguish between benign and malignant melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a hybrid deep feature extraction approach (combining HOG, LBP, DenseNet201, and ResNet50) and classification using multiple models (SVM, Random Forest, ANN, XGBoost, LightGBM) on a public dataset. It reports specific performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score) and proposes a novel methodology, which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Detection of Skin Cancer Through Dermoscopy Images Using Hybrid Deep Feature Extraction
journal: 2025 4th International Conference on Computing and Information Technology (ICCIT)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep feature extraction method for detecting skin cancer, specifically distinguishing between benign and malignant melanoma, using dermoscopy images. The researchers utilized a public Kaggle dataset containing 10,605 images and applied preprocessing techniques including morphological closing for hair removal and gamma correction. Feature extraction was performed using handcrafted methods (HOG, LBP) and deep learning models (DenseNet201, ResNet50), which were concatenated into hybrid feature sets. These features were classified using SVM, Random Forest, ANN, XGBoost, and LightGBM models. The best performance was achieved by combining HOG and DenseNet201 features with an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifier. The proposed model attained an accuracy of 98.80%, precision of 98.82%, recall of 98.69%, and an F1-score of 98.70%.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on detecting melanoma, specifically distinguishing between benign and malignant melanoma lesions. The dataset contains 5,105 images of malignant melanoma skin lesions.
The study classifies benign cases as the negative class for melanoma detection. The dataset contains 5,500 images of benign lesions/moles.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification task distinguishing between benign and malignant melanoma skin lesions using dermoscopy images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign or malignant) using classifiers such as SVM, Random Forest, ANN, XGBoost, and LightGBM on hybrid features (HOG, LBP, DenseNet201, ResNet50).

datasets_used.name: Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Images
datasets_used.description: A public dataset obtained from Kaggle containing dermoscopy images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training classification models (SVM, Random Forest, ANN, XGBoost, LightGBM) with hybrid features (HOG, LBP, DenseNet201, ResNet50). 85% of the data was used for training.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the paper mentions testing 15% of the data, implying the remaining 85% was used for training, but no specific validation set count or role is detailed.
use.testing: 15% of the dataset (1,591 images) was used for testing. The confusion matrix reports 815 true positives for benign and 756 true positives for malignant, with 10 false positives (benign predicted as malignant) and 9 false negatives (malignant predicted as benign).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on a confusion matrix.

Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Im.notes: The paper states that 'All models give efficient results' and 'All the combinations give good and efficient results,' but only provides specific numerical metrics for the best-performing model (HOG + DenseNet201 with ANN). The confusion matrix for the testing set shows: 815 True Positives (Benign correctly predicted), 10 False Positives (Benign predicted as Malignant), 756 True Positives (Malignant correctly predicted), and 9 False Negatives (Malignant predicted as Benign). Note: The text refers to benign as positive and malignant as negative in the confusion matrix description context, or vice versa depending on class labeling, but explicitly lists counts for correct predictions and errors.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Deep Feature Extraction with Machine Learning Classifiers
architecture_used.pretraining: DenseNet201 and ResNet50 used as feature extractors (implies ImageNet pretraining, though not explicitly stated in the provided text).

feature_extraction_methods: Handcrafted: Histogram Oriented Gradient (HOG), Local Binary Patterns (LBP)
architecture_details.feature_combination: Concatenation of features from different extractors into a single array
architecture_details.best_performing_combination: HOG + DenseNet201 features classified by ANN

Gamma correction (gamma value 1.2) for image enhancement

preprocessing_techniques: Morphological closing (structuring element size 10) for hair removal
Gamma correction (gamma value 1.2) for image enhancement
conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep feature extraction method, combining handcrafted features (HOG, LBP) and deep learning features (DenseNet201, ResNet50), effectively distinguishes between benign and malignant melanoma in dermoscopy images.
The combination of HOG and DenseNet201 features classified by an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) achieved the best performance among all tested models.
The best-performing model (HOG + DenseNet201 + ANN) attained an accuracy of 98.80%, precision of 98.82%, recall of 98.69%, and an F1-score of 98.70%.
All tested classifiers (SVM, Random Forest, ANN, XGBoost, LightGBM) provided efficient results, but ANN with the HOG and DenseNet201 feature combination was superior.
The study demonstrates that hybrid feature extraction can improve diagnostic accuracy for skin cancer detection compared to using single feature types.
The authors acknowledge that the model's generalization needs to be verified on different available datasets in future work.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single public Kaggle dataset ('Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Images') with 10,605 images, raising concerns about dataset bias and generalizability to diverse clinical populations.
The authors explicitly acknowledge in the conclusion that the model's generalization needs to be verified on different available datasets in future work, indicating a lack of external validation.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; performance was evaluated solely using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

The paper does not report individual performance metrics for all tested classifier combinations (SVM, Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM), only highlighting the best-performing model (HOG + DenseNet201 + ANN), which limits comparative analysis.
The dataset split is described as 85% training and 15% testing, but no separate validation set is mentioned, increasing the risk of overfitting or biased performance estimation.
Preprocessing steps include morphological closing for hair removal and gamma correction, but data augmentation techniques are not reported, which may limit robustness.

The study focuses exclusively on binary classification (benign vs. malignant melanoma) and does not address other common skin cancers like Basal Cell Carcinoma or Squamous Cell Carcinoma, limiting clinical scope.
Implementation details such as optimizer, loss function, hyperparameters, and training schedule are not reported, hindering reproducibility.
